chapters.title: Embrace balance: Less stress, more joy
chapters.text:
Brigid Schulte was part of an internal group at the Washington Post that researched why only a few women read the newspapers. Their study revealed that only women in retirement have time to read the papers. In contrast, women between the ages of 18 and 49 seemed to be so encumbered with many things like house chores, taking care of the kids, and trying to meet deadlines at work that a newspaper subscription wasn’t a priority.

Schulte was also responsible for gathering data on how women used their time to reveal how busy they were. She started her research by Google searching “time busy women,” and John Robinson's name popped up. Robinson happened to be a sociologist who had spent about 50 years studying the way people spend their time.

Schulte called Robinson and told him that she felt women were too busy to read the papers. He replied in the negative, asserting that women have a minimum of 30 hours of free time weekly. This came as a shock to Schulte, as she recalled all the stress she went through on a typical day, days when she felt so overwhelmed, she could hardly breathe. Sometimes, she stayed up late to finish her son’s school project, bake valentine cakes, or write stories. She often conducted official interviews in unconventional places like sitting on the floor outside her children's dentist's office, in school bathrooms during events, and from her car. Robinson later invited her to go on a time study with him, and this invitation led to many discoveries and insights into how people spend their time. The study revealed how men and women need to create time for leisure if they desire to live their best lives.

Take time out to catch a breath amid the hustle and bustle of work for the sake of your mental health.

This summary aims to teach you how much you have devalued your leisure time by burying yourself in work to the extent that you no longer value time alone. You will learn that it’s okay not to be perfect but ensure you always give your best and do the little things that count.

Time is the coin of your life. You spend it. Do not allow others to spend it for you. ~ Carl Sandburg

chapters.title: Escape the trap of busyness, find time for you
chapters.text:
Brigid Schulte was reluctant to accept John Robinson’s offer to go on a time study. She felt she could prove that women didn’t have 30 hours of free time in a week. But then, she remembered that she hadn’t been a good time manager since childhood. Schulte recalled when she constantly ran out the door with shoes and a toothbrush in hand to get to school or church on time; working and being a mother also seemed to be a time-consuming task.

Leisure should be anyone’s greatest priority because it reduces your stress level.

Schulte wanted to be sure that her overwhelming life wasn’t a consequence of her weakness, so she reached out to her friends to learn about their leisure time. She discovered that she was not the only one who seemed to believe that the average mother has no free time. For instance, a friend admitted that if she found herself with free time, she probably would spend it feeling anxious about what she was forgetting. Eventually, she agreed to go on the study with John Robinson, where she had to keep a time diary that tracked her time for 18 months. When Robinson analyzed her diary, he discovered that she had 28 hours of leisure in a week. Robinson invited her to The International Association for Time Use Research (IATUR) in Paris to answer how people perfectly combine work, love, and play. At the conference, she met several time researchers who gave reports on role overload in both men and women. These researchers revealed that the feeling of being overwhelmed was on the rise, especially among women, and their major goal was to find out how people could get relief from it. The IATUR researchers hoped to find the keys to time serenity, where people can effectively combine work, love, and play.

chapters.title: Making time for rest is a decision, and it is the key to a good life
chapters.text:
After the conference in Paris, Schulte dashed to a seminar in the Netherlands in a bid to find out why it’s challenging to combine work, love, and play. She had conversations with some of the researchers and discovered that she wouldn’t understand why people had no time for leisure until she paid attention to the major factors that were killing time, work, and love. Here is what some researchers had to say about these time killers:

• Work

Sociologists Michael Hout and Caroline Hanley noted that in the late 1960s, 38% of mothers with young children had jobs, mostly part-time. By the 2000s, this rose to 75% in full-time roles. They found that by 2000, working parents were putting in 13 more hours per week than in 1970, totaling an extra 676 hours, or about 28 days, per year. This figure also takes into account the time spent on child care and household tasks. Hout and Hanley’s research suggests that modern working families are indeed working more.

• Love

UCLA time-use researcher Suzanne Bianchi told Schulte that most parents don’t feel they are doing enough and are pressured to increase the hours they spend with their kids. Mothers often select jobs that allow for more flexible or reduced hours, sacrificing career progression. They typically minimize housework, self-care time, and moments with partners to manage their schedules. They also sleep less and give up time to play. As a result, they find it difficult to have uninterrupted leisure, overwhelmed with meeting everyone’s needs. For instance, if a mother is at the pool, she will be on the constant lookout for her children instead of relaxing.

Take out time to just have fun even if you have to let go of other priorities; this will give you a moment of peace.

chapters.title: Mothers at work: Know your worth and rights
chapters.text:
Brigid Schulte had many unanswered questions after the conference and the seminar. She sought stories of people who recognized the importance of leisure, leading her to Renate Rivelli, a 39-year-old single mother of two.

Rivelli worked as a benefits manager at the Human Resources Department of Brown Palace Hotel in Denver. Although she loved her job, the hotel didn’t have a family-friendly policy to help employees manage work and life demands. She frequently worked until midnight for months, covering for an absent colleague. This dedication earned her high performance reviews and the title of Manager of the Year in 2005. So, it was unexpected when, in 2008, she was informed of a new boss in her department, a younger, less experienced colleague without children. Rivelli was upset, feeling overlooked for the position. The managers said the new position would be very demanding with more hours, a lot of traveling, and a possible relocation; hence they didn’t think she would be up for it because she was a mother.

Rivelli filed a claim with the Equal Opportunity Employment Commission and was successful in her case. Two years later, she had earned a degree and worked in a new company where telecommuting and flexible working hours were the norms. As a result, she now has more time for family and leisure because she has more flexible hours.

Equality in the workplace creates a conducive work environment where everyone feels secure and comfortable.

Renate Rivelli’s case has shown that even work contributes to most women feeling overwhelmed. The workplace expects everyone to be ideal workers who let go of all home duties and only focus on work. Companies need to value family responsibilities and put measures in place to not discriminate against individuals who need to attend to such obligations.

chapters.title: Workplaces where being you equals success
chapters.text:
Although many workplaces still hold on to the ideal worker mentality, things are changing. The ideal worker norm is being overthrown, and people are committing to live more balanced lives at work, love, and play. An example is Menlo Innovations, whose co-founder and CEO is Rich Sheridan. Menlo Innovations expects people to be in the office but puts strict boundaries on their time there, such as you can even be fired for working overtime. Sheridan founded his company with “joy” as its core principle, believing in autonomy, mastery, and purpose as motivators for creativity and productivity, rather than extended work hours.

Spending overtime at work generally leads to burnout.

Rich Sheridan designed Menlo Innovations to promote concentrated work, collaboration, and creativity; there are no private offices or earbuds. Instead, weekly, workers are paired together and assigned to work on one project at a time to encourage focus. There is no multitasking at Menlo Innovations, and workers are permitted to work anywhere and in whatever manner they like in the company. Sheridan highlighted that working in pairs and assigning tasks are beneficial in the following ways:

• Frees workers from decision fatigue of what to do, when, or who to do it with
• Creates time for everyone such that no one is indispensable and can take breaks when needed, and, still, the work will continue

The longest meeting in the company lasts for 10 minutes, and everyone leaves at closing hour. Workers are permitted to bring their kids to work and breastfeed during work. Menlo Innovations is recognized for its innovative, democratic, and creative environment, even receiving accolades from the American Psychological Association for its psychologically healthy workplace. Beyond its culture, the company is also financially successful, growing significantly since its inception in 2001.

The best workplaces recognize that working in a new way requires learning new skills and not assuming people automatically just know how to work with flexibility. ~ Brigid Schulte

chapters.title: Despite your busy schedule, learn to do what you are passionate about
chapters.text:
Nobody can change time; there will always be 24 hours a day and 168 hours in a week. You can only control how you allocate your time to different activities. People who are busy need to know that it’s impossible to always do what you think you need to do. For example, when people die, their email inbox will still be full, and the to-do list will still be there. But they won’t be there to do the tasks. So, you must get to that point where you decide what you want to accomplish in life.

Know your priorities and what is currently important to you, then focus on them.

Brigid Schulte was at a Time Triage workshop where people were asked by Terry Monaghan, a productivity expert, what they would do if they had free time. Schulte replied that she would read; others said travel, sleep, pray, etc. Then Monaghan asked them to make room for that on their schedules, starting with the important things. That was where Schulte discovered that she didn’t have a scale of priority; everything seemed important to her. Ellen Ernst Kossek, an organizational psychologist, explained this when she described that there are two kinds of people when it comes to work and home duties:

• Integrators see everything, including work and family as important and tend to do everything all at once every time.
• Separators distinctly divide their professional and personal lives.

Kossek called Schulte not just an integrator but a “fusion lover.” Fusion lovers often struggle with decision-making and prioritizing, leading to a lack of clear focus. Schulte realized that she couldn’t have it all and needed to be in a position where there was room for work, love, and play. At first, it wasn’t easy for her to change her schedule and life, but she learned how to work in pulses through time chunking. This reduced constant multitasking and role switching and thus, resulted in more leisure time. Did you know? According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, in 2019, men spent 5.5 hours per day in leisure activities while women spent just 4.9 hours.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
One important thing to note is that you need to learn how to fully focus on the present to overcome that life of busyness. Neuroscientists at the Yale Stress Center discovered that the more we get overwhelmed with a lot of things, the faster our brain tends to shrink. It's important to consciously slow down, take breaks, and appreciate our surroundings to feel more grounded. The truth is being busy or overwhelmed doesn’t go away, but we can choose how we react to it. We can pause to notice it without being judgmental or reacting to it at all. Being mindful makes you concentrate on what’s happening in your life currently without thinking of rushing to the next moment.

Train yourself to enjoy little things like listening to music and watching the sunset. Don’t be so anxious about doing the next thing that you miss out on in life. The only way to achieve this is to make time for leisure. Every moment of your life is precious, so find time to do things that you enjoy. Life is very short, so make every day count. Take a break from things you need to take a break from and spend time doing things and with people that truly matter. Also, we all need to learn a new way to work. This doesn’t mean working less but working smart. The workplace needs to embrace a healthier work environment where there is equality and more opportunities for everyone. It needs to be a place where it is okay to be a mother and okay for fathers to take parental leave to take care of things at home. This should be the goal of every employer and employee.

Try this

Take some time to pause for three to five minutes before doing the next thing. You can spend those few minutes smiling, taking a deep breath, exercising, taking a walk, or doing something that makes you feel relaxed.

Read it till the end?
